title: 10 best herbal teas for stomach pain
intro: If you're experiencing stomach pain, certain herbal teas can offer relief by reducing inflammation, soothing digestion, and alleviating cramps. Peppermint tea is well-known for its calming effects on the gastrointestinal tract, making it an excellent choice for easing stomach discomfort. Ginger tea is another popular option, as it contains anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the stomach lining and alleviate pain. Chamomile tea has long been used as a natural remedy for various ailments, including stomach pain, due to its soothing and antispasmodic qualities, which can help relax the digestive muscles and decrease tension. Lastly, licorice root tea may be beneficial for treating stomach pain caused by gastric ulcers, as it promotes the healing of the stomach lining and reduces inflammation. However, it is important to consult your healthcare provider before consuming licorice root tea, as excessive intake can lead to side effects such as high blood pressure and potassium depletion.

teas.tea_desc: Chamomile tea contains antispasmodic and anti-inflammatory compounds, such as chamazulene and flavonoids, which directly target the gastrointestinal tract. These properties help relax the smooth muscles in the stomach, reducing spasms and inflammation that cause pain. Furthermore, chamomile's calming effects extend to the nervous system, alleviating stress-related stomach issues. Additionally, chamomile's mild astringent qualities can help with leakage from the gastrointestinal lining, commonly seen in conditions like acid reflux. Lastly, chamomile tea promotes the production of mucin, a glycoprotein that forms a protective layer in the stomach, preventing direct acid contact with the stomach lining and thereby reducing pain.
tea_parts: Flowers: Chamomile's daisy-like flowers are rich in aromatic compounds and antispasmodic properties, making them ideal for soothing stomach pain.
Leaves: The delicate, feathery leaves contain calming agents that help reduce inflammation and alleviate discomfort in the digestive tract.
Seeds: Chamomile seeds are a powerhouse of essential oils and therapeutic properties, easing gastrointestinal spasms and providing quick relief for stomach pain.
tea_recipe: Boil 1 cup of water and let it cool for 2-3 minutes until it reaches a temperature of around 200°F (93°C).
Place 1-2 teaspoons of dried chamomile flowers in a tea infuser or teapot.
Pour the hot water over the chamomile flowers and let it steep for 5-10 minutes.
Remove the tea infuser or strain the tea to discard the chamomile flowers.
Drink the chamomile tea slowly, ideally without added sweeteners, to soothe the stomach pain.
tea_constituents: Apigenin: A flavonoid that has anti-spasmodic properties, helping to relax the smooth muscles of the digestive tract and alleviate stomach pain.
Bisabolol: A terpene with anti-inflammatory and anti-spasmodic effects, which can help reduce inflammation and spasms in the gastrointestinal tract, thus alleviating stomach pain.
Chamazulene: A chemical compound with strong anti-inflammatory properties, which can help decrease inflammation in the digestive tract, providing relief from stomach pain.
Matricin: A precursor to chamazulene, matricin also possesses anti-inflammatory effects, aiding in the reduction of inflammation in the gastrointestinal tract and easing stomach pain.
Alpha-Bisabolol Oxide A & B: Oxides of bisabolol with soothing and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help alleviate pain and inflammation in the stomach.

teas.tea_desc: Peppermint tea aids stomach pain due to its active ingredient, menthol, which boasts antispasmodic properties. This compound relaxes the gastrointestinal muscles, reducing discomfort and cramping. Furthermore, peppermint oil, found in the tea, increases bile production, promoting healthy digestion and nutrient absorption. Consequently, this herbal remedy alleviates bloating and gas build-up, providing swift relief for minor stomach ailments. Ultimately, peppermint tea offers a natural, caffeine-free solution for those seeking solace from occasional stomach pain.
tea_parts: Leaves: Rich in volatile oils, leaves are commonly used in medicinal tea for stomach pain due to their antispasmodic and calming properties.
Flowers: Containing therapeutic compounds, flowers provide soothing relief for stomach pain through their anti-inflammatory and calming effects.
Seeds: Packed with beneficial nutrients, seeds are used in medicinal tea for stomach pain because they help reduce spasms and promote digestive health.
tea_recipe: Crush 1 teaspoon of dried peppermint leaves or use 1 peppermint tea bag.
Place the crushed leaves or tea bag in a tea infuser or directly into a cup.
Boil 8 ounces of water and pour it over the peppermint leaves or tea bag.
Let the tea steep for 5-10 minutes to allow the peppermint oils to release.
Remove the tea bag or infuser, and enjoy the hot tea to soothe your stomach pain.
tea_constituents: Menthol: The main active ingredient in peppermint, menthol has antispasmodic properties that help relax the gastrointestinal tract, providing relief from stomach pain.
Menthone: This constituent also contributes to peppermint's antispasmodic effects, further alleviating symptoms of stomach pain and discomfort.
Limonene: A compound with anti-inflammatory properties, limonene may help reduce inflammation in the stomach lining, thereby providing relief from pain and irritation.
Menthol acetate: This compound exhibits analgesic properties, making it effective in relieving pain in the gastrointestinal tract.
Rosmarinic acid: With anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects, rosmarinic acid can help protect the stomach lining, reducing the risk of stomach pain and promoting overall digestive health.

teas.tea_desc: Ginger tea brings immediate relief from stomach pain because ginger contains gingerols and shogaols, powerful healing compounds. These substances work by reducing inflammation in the gut and relaxing the stomach muscles, thus alleviating pain and cramping. Furthermore, ginger tea aids digestion by increasing digestive enzyme secretion and calming nausea, addressing two common causes of stomach discomfort. Additionally, ginger's antimicrobial properties help fight off harmful bacteria in the digestive tract that can cause pain and inflammation. Ultimately, ginger tea's soothing warmth gently massages the stomach, providing additional comfort and fostering rapid healing.
tea_parts: Roots: The roots of the ginger plant are used to make medicinal tea for stomach pain due to their natural anti-inflammatory properties.
Rhizomes: Ginger rhizomes are rich in phenolic compounds that aid in digestion, making them an effective ingredient in medicinal teas for soothing stomach pain.
Leaves: Ginger leaves contain essential oils that help relax the gastrointestinal muscles, providing relief from stomach pain.
Flowers: Ginger flowers have polyphenols which possess gastroprotective qualities, making them suitable for use in medicinal teas to alleviate stomach discomfort.
Seeds: Ginger seeds are a potent source of antioxidants and have been found to provide relief from digestive ailments, such as stomach pain.
tea_recipe: Peel and grate 1 tablespoon of fresh ginger, then let it steep in 1 cup of hot water for 5 minutes.
Strain the ginger pieces from the water using a fine mesh sieve or cheesecloth.
Squeeze the juice of half a lemon into the ginger water.
Stir in 1-2 teaspoons of honey, adjusting the amount based on your preference for sweetness.
Drink the tea while it's still warm, up to three times a day for stomach pain relief.
tea_constituents: Gingerols: Gingerols have anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce stomach inflammation and pain.
Shogaols: Shogaols can help relax the smooth muscle of the gastrointestinal tract, alleviating stomach cramps and pain.
Paradols: Paradols have been shown to help reduce nausea and vomiting, common symptoms associated with stomach pain.
Zingibain: Zingibain can help break down proteins in the stomach, aiding in digestion and reducing stomach discomfort.
Gingerdiol: Gingerdiol has been found to help inhibit the growth of harmful bacteria in the stomach, preventing infection and reducing stomach pain.

teas.tea_desc: Licorice tea provides relief from stomach pain due to its active compounds, including glycyrrhizin, which possess anti-inflammatory and mucilage-like properties. These substances create a protective barrier along the stomach lining, preventing acid and digestive enzymes from causing further irritation. Moreover, licorice tea stimulates the production of mucus, adding an extra layer of protection that soothes and alleviates discomfort. Consequently, consuming licorice tea leads to reduced stomach pain, offering a natural and effective remedy for various gastrointestinal ailments.
tea_parts: Roots: The roots of the licorice plant contain glycyrrhizin, a compound with anti-inflammatory properties that can help soothe stomach lining and reduce pain.
Rhizomes: Licorice rhizomes are rich in flavonoids that promote gastric mucus production, providing a protective barrier against stomach acids.
Leaves: The leaves of the licorice plant have been used in traditional medicine to treat stomach pain due to their carminative properties, which help relieve bloating and gas.
Seeds: Licorice seeds contain various antioxidants and have been used in herbal medicine to alleviate stomach discomfort and promote digestion.
Flowers: Although not as commonly used, licorice flowers can be used in medicinal teas for stomach pain due to their soothing effects on the gastrointestinal tract.
tea_recipe: In a teapot, combine 1 teaspoon of dried licorice root with 8 ounces of boiling water.
Steep the tea for 10-15 minutes to allow the active compounds to fully extract.
Strain the tea to remove the licorice root pieces.
Optionally, add honey or lemon to taste.
Drink the tea while it is still warm, up to three times a day as needed.
tea_constituents: Glycyrrhizin: This compound has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce stomach lining irritation and alleviate pain.
Liquiritigenin: It has been shown to inhibit gastric acid secretion, which can help with symptoms of stomach pain and acid reflux.
Glabridin: With antibacterial properties, glabridin can help combat H. pylori, a common cause of stomach ulcers and pain.
Liquiritin: This flavonoid helps increase mucus production in the stomach, protecting the mucosal lining and providing relief from stomach pain.
Glycyrrhetic acid: It can help improve gastric mucosal defense and healing of stomach ulcers, providing relief from stomach pain.

teas.tea_desc: Fennel tea provides immediate relief from stomach pain due to its antispasmodic and carminative properties. This herbal tea relaxes the smooth muscles of the gastrointestinal tract, reducing the intensity and frequency of stomach contractions that cause pain. Moreover, it encourages the expulsion of built-up gas, alleviating bloating and discomfort. Additionally, fennel's volatile oils stimulate the production of digestive enzymes, promoting a smooth and efficient digestive process, and thus preventing stomach pain.
tea_parts: Roots: The roots of fennel contain compounds that have been found to reduce inflammation in the stomach lining, making it effective for soothing stomach pain.
Seeds: Fennel seeds are rich in antispasmodic properties which help relax the gastrointestinal muscles, providing relief from stomach cramps and pain.
Leaves: The leaves of fennel plants have calming effects on the digestive system, making them useful in alleviating symptoms of stomach pain.
tea_recipe: Chop one fennel plant (including seeds) into small pieces, then rinse under cold water.
Place fennel pieces in a pot and pour 4 cups of water.
Bring the pot to a boil, then reduce heat and let it simmer for 15 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove fennel pieces, then let it cool for 5 minutes.
Pour the tea into a cup and drink it slowly to soothe stomach pain.
tea_constituents: Anethole: This compound has anti-spasmodic properties, which can help relax the gastrointestinal muscles and alleviate stomach pain.
Fenchone: Fenchone has carminative effects, which means it can help reduce gas and bloating in the stomach, thereby easing pain.
Limonene: This compound has anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce inflammation in the stomach lining and alleviate pain.
Estragole: Estragole can help stimulate the production of digestive enzymes, which can improve digestion and reduce stomach pain.
Alpha-pinene: This compound has analgesic properties, which can help reduce pain and discomfort in the stomach.

teas.tea_desc: Basil tea brings relief to stomach pain due to its carminative properties, which facilitate the expulsion of intestinal gas, reducing discomfort and bloating. The tea's anti-inflammatory compounds, including eugenol and rosmarinic acid, address stomach pain by reducing inflammation in the gastrointestinal tract. Basil tea's essential oils, such as linalool and citronellol, boast antispasmodic qualities that help inhibit smooth muscle spasms in the gut, thus alleviating stomach pain. Furthermore, basil tea's soothing warmth increases blood flow and relaxes the abdominal muscles, which can ease digestion and consequently alleviate stomach pain. The phenolic acids in basil tea display remarkable antioxidant capabilities, protecting the gastrointestinal system from oxidative stress and preventing stomach pain.
tea_parts: Leaves: The leaves of basil are rich in volatile oils and flavonoids, known to help reduce inflammation and provide relief from stomach cramps and spasms.
Seeds: Basil seeds contain essential oils and mucilage, which have anti-inflammatory and soothing properties, offering comfort to an upset stomach.
Flowers: High in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds, basil flowers help soothe the digestive system and alleviate symptoms of stomach pain.
tea_recipe: In a small pot, boil 2 cups of water for 5 minutes.
Add 2 teaspoons of dried basil leaves to the boiling water, then reduce heat and let it simmer for 3-5 minutes.
Remove the pot from heat, cover, and let it steep for an additional 5 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the basil leaves.
Drink the tea while warm, up to three times a day for stomach pain relief.
tea_constituents: Eugenol: This compound has anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties that can help relieve pain and inflammation in the stomach.
Methyl chavicol: Also known as estragole, it has anti-spasmodic effects that can help reduce muscle spasms in the gastrointestinal tract, alleviating stomach pain.
Orientin: This flavonoid has been shown to have gastro-protective effects, which can help prevent and treat stomach ulcers and inflammation.
Vicenin-2: Another flavonoid, vicenin-2 has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that can help protect the stomach lining and reduce damage caused by free radicals.
Ursolic acid: This compound has been found to have anti-ulcer effects, helping to prevent and treat stomach ulcers.

teas.tea_desc: Coriander tea alleviates stomach pain due to its carminative properties, which derive from its essential oils. These oils prompt the expulsion of gas from the digestive tract, thus reducing bloating and cramps. Moreover, coriander's antispasmodic agents soothe the smooth muscle lining of the stomach, further alleviating discomfort. Additionally, this herbal infusion boasts anti-inflammatory compounds, which help lessen irritation in the gastrointestinal tract, providing additional relief.
tea_parts: Roots: The roots of coriander are used in medicinal tea for stomach pain due to their anti-inflammatory and calming properties.
Seeds: The seeds of coriander are used in medicinal tea for stomach pain due to their carminative and anti-spasmodic properties.
Leaves: The leaves of coriander are used in medicinal tea for stomach pain due to their aromatic and settling properties.
tea_recipe: In a pot, boil 2 cups of water for 5 minutes.
Add 1 tablespoon of dried coriander seeds, reduce heat, and simmer for 10 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the seeds.
Optional: Add honey or lemon to taste.
Drink the tea while it's still warm, up to three times a day for stomach pain relief.
tea_constituents: Borneol: Has anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties that can help alleviate pain in the stomach.
Linalool: Exhibits antispasmodic and anti-inflammatory effects, helping to reduce stomach cramps and inflammation.
Camphor: Has carminative properties that can help relieve gas and bloating in the stomach, thus reducing pain.
Geraniol: Displays antioxidant and gastroprotective effects, helping to protect the stomach lining and reduce pain.
Citronellol: Possesses antispasmodic and anti-inflammatory properties, aiding in the reduction of stomach pain and inflammation.

teas.tea_desc: Dandelion tea aids stomach pain due to its proven medicinal properties. This tea, derived from the dandelion plant, contains potent compounds that stimulate digestion and soothe the stomach. Dandelion's bitter components trigger the release of stomach juices, promoting efficient digestion and alleviating discomfort. Additionally, its anti-inflammatory properties reduce inflammation in the gut, further easing pain. Furthermore, dandelion tea's richness in antioxidants helps in repairing damaged stomach tissues, contributing to overall stomach health.
tea_parts: Roots: The roots of dandelions contain a variety of beneficial compounds, including inulin and sesquiterpene lactones, which can aid in digestion and reduce inflammation in the stomach.
Leaves: Dandelion leaves are rich in vitamins and minerals, and possess diuretic properties that can help regulate stomach moisture and soothe digestive issues.
Flowers: Dandelion flowers contain antioxidants and can stimulate the production of digestive juices, making them useful for easing stomach pain.
Buds: The unopened flower buds of dandelions are a good source of potassium and can help reduce inflammation in the stomach lining.
tea_recipe: Gather 2-3 dandelion heads and rinse them thoroughly under running water to remove dirt and insects.
Separate the yellow petals from the green base and calyxes, then chop the petals finely for faster brewing.
Place the dandelion petals in a teapot or infuser and add 1 cup (250 ml) of boiling water; steep for 5-10 minutes to extract the medicinal compounds.
Strain the dandelion tea to remove the solid particles, then add honey or lemon to taste, if desired, for added flavor and soothing properties.
Consume the tea while it's still warm, 2-3 times a day, as needed, to alleviate stomach pain and promote digestive health.
tea_constituents: Taraxacin: This medicinal constituent in dandelion tea stimulates digestion and appetite, which can help alleviate stomach pain caused by indigestion or lack of appetite.
Taraxacerin: Taraxacerin can help reduce inflammation in the stomach, which can help reduce stomach pain and discomfort.
Inulin: Inulin is a type of dietary fiber found in dandelion tea that can help regulate the digestive system, reducing symptoms of stomach pain and discomfort.
Sesquiterpene Lactones: These medicinal constituents have been shown to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which can help reduce stomach pain and protect the stomach from damage.
Phenolic Acids: Phenolic acids in dandelion tea have been shown to have gastroprotective effects, helping to reduce stomach pain and prevent stomach ulcers.
Volatile Oils: The volatile oils in dandelion tea, including monoterpenes and sesquiterpenes, can help stimulate the production of digestive enzymes, reducing stomach pain and improving digestion.
Potassium: Dandelion tea is high in potassium, which can help regulate fluid balance in the body, reducing stomach pain and bloating caused by fluid retention.
Tannins: Tannins in dandelion tea have astringent properties, which can help reduce inflammation and soothe the digestive tract, reducing stomach pain and discomfort.

teas.tea_desc: Marshmallow tea, derived from the marshmallow plant's root, offers stomach pain relief due to its substantial mucilage content. This mucilage transforms into a thick, slippery substance upon contact with water, forming a soothing coating that shields the stomach's lining. As a result, this protective layer alleviates stomach pain by reducing inflammation and irritation. Moreover, the tea's antioxidant properties further contribute to calming the stomach, creating an overall relaxing effect. Ultimately, the demulcent and anti-inflammatory features of marshmallow tea make it an excellent choice for soothing stomach pain.
tea_parts: Roots: The roots contain soothing properties that help alleviate stomach pain.
Leaves: The leaves have anti-inflammatory compounds that aid in reducing stomach discomfort.
Flowers: The flowers possess calming elements that help soothe an upset stomach.
Bark: The bark contains therapeutic substances that help relieve stomach aches.
tea_recipe: In a saucepan, combine 2 cups of water, 1 tablespoon of dried marshmallow root, and 1 teaspoon of grated ginger; simmer for 15 minutes.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve into a teapot or mug, pressing on the solids to extract all liquid.
Discard the marshmallow root and ginger solids.
Add honey to taste, if desired, and stir until dissolved.
Serve the tea hot, and sip slowly to soothe the stomach pain.
tea_constituents: Mucilage: The mucilage in marshmallow tea coats the stomach lining, protecting it from irritants and soothing stomach pain.
Flavonoids: The flavonoids in marshmallow tea have anti-inflammatory properties, helping to reduce inflammation in the stomach lining and alleviate pain.
Polysaccharides: The polysaccharides in marshmallow tea provide a soothing, demulcent effect on the stomach lining, helping to relieve pain and discomfort.
Phenolic acids: The phenolic acids in marshmallow tea have antioxidant properties, helping to protect the stomach lining from damage and reducing pain.

teas.tea_desc: Slippery elm tea addresses stomach pain by creating a protective barrier in the gut. This tea's demulcent quality allows it to relieve irritation and soothe inflammation in the gastrointestinal tract. The Slippery elm's mucilage content, which becomes gel-like when mixed with water, forms a coating over the lining of the stomach. Consequently, this coating helps to alleviate pain by reducing exposure to stomach acids. Additionally, this herbal tea promotes the production of mucus within the gastrointestinal tract, further contributing to its ability to ease discomfort and promote digestive health.
tea_parts: Bark: The inner bark is rich in mucilage, providing a soothing and protective coating for inflamed digestive tissue.
Leaves: The leaves contain mucilage, known to ease discomfort and support stomach health.
Buds: The buds are a concentrated source of mucilage, making them particularly effective at soothing stomach pain.
Roots: The roots also contain mucilage, offering a comforting and protective layer for the digestive tract.
tea_recipe: Measure - Scoop 1 teaspoon of slippery elm bark powder into a tea infuser or mesh strainer.
Boil - Bring 8 ounces of water to a boil in a saucepan or kettle.
Steep - Submerge the slippery elm bark in the boiling water and steep for 5-10 minutes.
Strain - Remove the tea infuser or strain the tea into a cup, discarding the slippery elm bark solids.
Sip - Drink the slippery elm tea while warm, up to three times daily as needed for stomach pain relief.
tea_constituents: Mucilage: Slippery elm tea contains mucilage, a substance that becomes a slick gel when mixed with water. This gel can provide a protective barrier to the digestive tract, helping to soothe stomach pain.
Tannins: Slippery elm tea contains tannins, which have astringent properties. These tannins can help to reduce inflammation and irritation in the stomach, providing relief from stomach pain.

Antioxidants: Slippery elm tea is rich in antioxidants, which can help to protect the body from damage caused by free radicals. These antioxidants can help to reduce inflammation in the stomach, providing relief from stomach pain.
Fiber: Slippery elm tea contains fiber, which can help to promote regularity and healthy digestion. This can help to alleviate stomach pain caused by constipation or other digestive issues.

Vitamins and Minerals: Slippery elm tea contains vitamins and minerals such as vitamin A, vitamin C, and calcium. These nutrients can help to support overall digestive health, which can help to reduce stomach pain.

other_remedies: Among the most common and effective natural remedies for stomach pain are heat therapy, ginger, and hydration. Heat therapy, such as applying a heating pad or hot water bottle to the abdomen, helps to relax the muscles and alleviate discomfort. Ginger, a well-known natural anti-inflammatory, can be consumed in various forms, including fresh, dried, or as an extract, to soothe the digestive tract and reduce nausea. Furthermore, staying hydrated by drinking plenty of water is crucial for maintaining optimal gut health and preventing stomach pain caused by dehydration.
causes: [Inflammation]: A condition in which the stomach lining becomes reddened and swollen, often due to infection by bacteria or irritation from alcohol, cigarettes, or nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs).
[Gastritis]: Stomach inflammation that causes symptoms like stomach pain, bloating, vomiting, and indigestion.

[Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease: A chronic condition that occurs when stomach acid flows back into your food pipe (esophagus), irritating the lining and causing symptoms like stomach pain, heartburn, and difficulty swallowing.
[Ulcers]: Open sores that develop on the lining of the stomach or small intestine, often caused by long-term use of NSAIDs or an infection by the bacterium Helicobacter pylori.
[Irritable Bowel Syndrome: A functional gastrointestinal disorder that affects the large intestine, causing symptoms like cramping, abdominal pain, bloating, gas, and diarrhea or constipation.
[Food Intolerance]: An adverse reaction to certain foods or ingredients that the body cannot digest or break down properly, leading to stomach pain, bloating, gas, and diarrhea.

[Gastroenteritis]: Also known as the stomach flu, an inflammation of the stomach and intestines caused by a viral or bacterial infection, resulting in symptoms like stomach pain, diarrhea, vomiting, and fever.

[Intestinal Obstruction]: A blockage in the small or large intestine that prevents food, fluids, and gas from passing through, leading to stomach pain, cramping, bloating, vomiting, and constipation or diarrhea.

[Diverticulitis]: An inflammation or infection of the small pouches (diverticula) that form in the lining of the digestive tract, often causing stomach pain, fever, nausea, and changes in bowel movements.
[Crohn's Disease]: A type of inflammatory bowel disease that causes inflammation in the digestive tract, often resulting in stomach pain, diarrhea, fatigue, and weight loss.
[Pancreatitis]: An inflammation of the pancreas, a gland that produces digestive enzymes and hormones, causing symptoms like stomach pain, nausea, vomiting, and fever.
[Endometriosis]: A condition in which tissue similar to the lining of the uterus grows outside the uterus, often causing stomach pain, heavy bleeding, and infertility.

[Pelvic Inflammatory Disease: An infection of the reproductive organs in women, often caused by sexually transmitted bacteria, resulting in stomach pain, fever, irregular menstruation, and painful urination.
[Ectopic Pregnancy]: A rare but serious condition in which a fertilized egg implants outside the uterus, often causing stomach pain, vaginal bleeding, and dizziness or fainting.
[Ovarian Cysts]: Fluid-filled sacs that develop on the ovaries, often causing symptoms like stomach pain, bloating, and irregular menstruation.
[Testicular Torsion]: A twisting of the spermatic cord that supplies blood to the testicle, often causing sudden and severe stomach pain, nausea, and vomiting.
[Appendicitis]: An inflammation of the appendix, a small pouch-like structure attached to the large intestine, resulting in sudden and severe stomach pain, nausea, vomiting, and fever.
